original_abstract:
This article revisits Rudolf Bultmann’s seminal 1941 essay, "New Testament and Mythology," on the occasion of the 130th anniversary of the theologian's birth. Despite Bultmann’s status as a towering figure in 20th-century biblical scholarship, his program of demythologization historically faced significant resistance and dismissal within the Afrikaans-speaking South African theological landscape. By returning to the original text now that the original controversy has subsided, this study seeks to offer a clear appraisal of Bultmann’s voice, free from the layers of polemical interpretation provided by his critics.

The paper outlines Bultmann’s core motivation: to make the New Testament kerygma accessible to the modern person, whose worldview is shaped by science and technology rather than the ancient three-tiered mythical cosmos. Central to this process is "existential interpretation"—the translation of mythological language regarding the cross, resurrection, and eschatology into terms that address human existence and self-understanding. The study further engages with traditional critiques concerning Bultmann’s definition of myth and his debt to Heideggerian philosophy. Ultimately, the article argues that Bultmann’s work remains profoundly relevant, suggesting that demythologization is not a process of historical reduction, but a necessary hermeneutical engagement that allows the living faith to speak to contemporary reality.
humanized_abstract:
The purpose of this article is to re-examine Bultmann’s 1941 lecture “New Testament and Mythology” in the centenary year of his birth, 130 years ago. Bultmann, arguably one of the leading New Testament scholars of the 20th century, whose demythologisation programme evoked numerous scholarly and theological reactions, was met with rejection and polemics in the Afrikaans academic context in South Africa. Revisiting Bultmann’s position by engaging with his original work 130 years after its publication is aimed at giving a dispassionate picture of Bultmann’s voice as the controversion raised by his critics over time can now be bridged.

I here concentrate on what appears to me to be Bultmann’s central motivation in the whole operation. A modern human being, for whom the world of scientific and technological discoveries is real, cannot organise his understanding of the world in the same way as did the ancient human being in his ‘three-storied mythical’ world. Bultmann’s business is therefore existential interpretation: turning the mythological language into a mode of speech which deals with man’s existence and understanding of himself. A discussion of the theory of myth which underlies Bultmann’s work, and of Bultmann’s direct indebtedness to the existential philosophy of Heidegger, forms part of the whole article. I contend that after allowing for differences of period and intellectual background, demythologization can remain an important method of exposition and exegesis of the New Testament kerygma, because, while it by no means is simply a historical reduction of the text, it is always a necessary part of the hermeneutics of the living faith in a changing world.
